items.title: Commit an InitialCreate migration instead of relying on EnsureCreated
items.description: The generated ERP core must ship a checked-in EF Core migration history whose first migration (InitialCreate) builds the full deterministic schema of roughly 65 tables. Runtime EnsureCreated() must not be the production schema path: it bypasses the migrations table, cannot evolve an existing database, and silently diverges from a versioned history. The committed migration is the single source of truth for the relational shape.
items.applicability: Emitted once for the deterministic part of any generated ERP that targets a real SQL Server database and must be upgradable over time.
items.example: tools/LocalAIFactory.Generator/templates/erp-core ships Migrations/<timestamp>_InitialCreate.cs plus an AppDbContextModelSnapshot.cs; the host calls Database.Migrate() on boot rather than EnsureCreated().
items.limitation: Implementation implication: generate the migration through `dotnet ef migrations add InitialCreate` and commit both the migration and the model snapshot so the history is reproducible. Test implication: assert a migration named InitialCreate exists and that __EFMigrationsHistory is populated after startup. A committed InitialCreate covers only the deterministic, template-fixed model; modules generated non-deterministically per project still need follow-up migrations.

items.title: Provide an IDesignTimeDbContextFactory so the EF CLI builds the context without the host
items.description: The data project must expose a class implementing IDesignTimeDbContextFactory<AppDbContext> that constructs the DbContext from a design-time connection string. This lets `dotnet ef` add and script migrations without starting the web application, so the CLI does not run hosted services, bind ports, or require external services to be up at design time.
items.applicability: Any generated project where migrations are authored from the command line and the runtime host has startup side effects that must not fire during design-time tooling.
items.example: DesignTimeDbContextFactory.CreateDbContext(args) builds new DbContextOptionsBuilder<AppDbContext>().UseSqlServer(designConnString) and returns new AppDbContext(options); `dotnet ef migrations add X` then works headlessly.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the design-time connection string distinct and harmless (a local or env-driven value), never a production secret committed to source. Test implication: run `dotnet ef migrations list` in CI to prove the factory resolves a context. The factory only serves tooling; it is not used at runtime and must not be mistaken for the runtime DI registration.

items.title: Reference Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design from the startup project
items.description: The startup (web) project must reference Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design so the EF Core tooling can discover the application's services and the design-time factory. Without this reference the CLI cannot bootstrap the context from the startup project and migration commands fail with a design-assembly error.
items.applicability: Generated solutions where migrations are authored against the data project but the startup project supplies configuration and the design-time entry point.
items.example: The Web .csproj contains a PackageReference to Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design with PrivateAssets=all; commands run as `dotnet ef migrations add X --project Data --startup-project Web`.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: mark the package PrivateAssets=all so design-time tooling does not flow as a transitive runtime dependency. Test implication: a CI step that invokes `dotnet ef` from the startup project proves the reference is wired. The reference enables tooling only; it adds no runtime behaviour and must not be assumed to provide migration application by itself.

items.title: Use Database.Migrate() on SQL Server and EnsureCreated() only for portable SQLite tests
items.description: The production boot path against SQL Server must call Database.Migrate() to apply the committed migration history idempotently. EnsureCreated() is reserved for ephemeral, throwaway SQLite databases used in fast portable tests, where a full schema is built from the model in-memory or in a temp file and discarded. The two paths must never be mixed against the same database.
items.applicability: Generated startup configuration and the test fixtures of an ERP core that runs on SQL Server in production and SQLite in CI.
items.example: Program startup: if provider is SqlServer call db.Database.Migrate(); a SQLite-backed integration test fixture calls db.Database.EnsureCreated() against a unique temp file then deletes it on dispose.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: branch on the active provider so Migrate() never runs against a SQLite test file and EnsureCreated() never touches the SQL Server database. Test implication: assert the SQL Server path records rows in __EFMigrationsHistory while the SQLite path does not. EnsureCreated() builds the current model only and applies no migration history, so it cannot validate that migrations themselves are correct.

items.title: Emit a non-destructive apply-migrations.ps1 operator script
items.description: Generate an apply-migrations.ps1 that runs `dotnet ef database update` against a target database to bring it to the latest migration. The script must be non-destructive: it only applies pending forward migrations and never drops or recreates the database, so an operator can upgrade a live environment safely.
items.applicability: Operations and deployment of a generated ERP where a human or pipeline applies schema upgrades to an existing database between releases.
items.example: apply-migrations.ps1 invokes dotnet ef database update --project Data --startup-project Web --connection $env:ConnString and prints the resulting migration list for confirmation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the script must surface (not swallow) the EF exit code so a failed update fails the deployment. Test implication: run it against a database one migration behind and assert it advances exactly to head. It applies forward migrations only; it does not back up the database first, so an operator backup step remains a separate, mandatory prerequisite.

items.title: Emit a generate-sql-script.ps1 producing an idempotent migrations script for DBA review
items.description: Generate a generate-sql-script.ps1 that runs `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` to emit a single SQL file a DBA can review and apply manually in environments where the application is not permitted to run schema changes directly. The idempotent flag guards every statement so the script is safe to re-run.
items.applicability: Regulated or DBA-gated deployments of a generated ERP where schema changes pass through manual review rather than automatic Database.Migrate().
items.example: generate-sql-script.ps1 writes out migrations.sql via dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent --output migrations.sql; a DBA inspects the guarded IF NOT EXISTS blocks before running it in production.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: always pass --idempotent so the script checks __EFMigrationsHistory before each step. Test implication: run the generated script twice against the same database and assert the second run is a no-op. The script reflects committed migrations only; ad-hoc manual schema edits made outside EF will not be represented and can cause drift.

items.title: Emit a setup-sql-express.ps1 that creates the database if absent and fails gracefully when SQL Express is missing
items.description: Generate a setup-sql-express.ps1 that prepares a local SQL Express instance: it creates the target database only if it does not already exist, and when the SQL Express service or instance cannot be reached it exits with clear setup guidance rather than a raw stack trace. This gives a first-run developer a deterministic local bootstrap.
items.applicability: Local developer onboarding for a generated ERP intended to run on a workstation SQL Express instance.
items.example: setup-sql-express.ps1 tests for the (localdb) or .\SQLEXPRESS instance, creates LocalAIFactory if missing, and on connection failure prints 'SQL Express not found — install it or set $env:ConnString' then exits non-zero.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: detect existence before creating so re-running the script is safe and idempotent. Test implication: run it twice and assert the second run reports the database already exists. It only provisions an empty database; the application's own Database.Migrate() on first boot still applies the schema, so the script is a precondition, not a replacement for migrations.

items.title: Generate migration-verification tests asserting presence and a valid create script
items.description: Generate automated tests that assert the migration history is present and coherent: a named InitialCreate migration is registered, the model can generate a create script without throwing, and that script references the core tables of the schema. These tests catch a missing or broken migration before it reaches a real database.
items.applicability: CI of a generated ERP core to protect the committed migration history from silent regression as the model evolves.
items.example: A test resolves the migrations assembly, asserts it contains an InitialCreate, calls the relational create-script generator, and asserts the output contains CREATE TABLE statements for accounts, gl_entries, and companies.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive these tests off the IMigrationsAssembly / model rather than a live database so they run fast and offline. Test implication: the assertions must name specific core tables so an accidental table drop is caught. The tests prove the migration produces a coherent schema, not that the schema is functionally correct for every business rule.

items.title: Warn about the EnsureCreated stale-schema pitfall on existing SQLite files
items.description: EnsureCreated() only creates a database when none exists; it never alters an existing one. If a SQLite test file from a previous model version is reused, EnsureCreated() returns success while leaving the old schema in place, producing confusing 'no such column' failures after a model change. The fix is to delete the file (or use migrations) so the schema is rebuilt from the current model.
items.applicability: Portable SQLite test fixtures of a generated ERP whose model changes between runs.
items.example: After adding a column, a test against a leftover test.db throws 'no such column: NewField'; the fixture is corrected to delete test.db on setup before calling EnsureCreated(), rebuilding the schema.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: give each test run a unique temp file or delete the file in fixture setup so EnsureCreated() always builds fresh. Test implication: assert the schema contains a newly added column to catch a stale file. This is inherent to EnsureCreated() and is exactly why production must use Migrate() — EnsureCreated() cannot evolve an existing schema at all.

items.title: Treat the committed InitialCreate as covering the deterministic model only
items.description: The committed InitialCreate captures the fixed, template-driven part of the model that is identical across every generated project. Any tables a project adds through non-deterministic, per-project generation (optional modules, customer-specific doctypes) are not in InitialCreate and must be introduced through additive follow-up migrations, keeping the history linear and reproducible.
items.applicability: Generated ERP cores that combine a fixed deterministic schema with optional or customer-specific modules layered on later.
items.example: InitialCreate builds the 65 core tables; enabling an optional manufacturing module triggers a follow-up AddManufacturing migration rather than editing InitialCreate after the fact.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never hand-edit InitialCreate to add later tables; always append a new migration so existing databases can upgrade. Test implication: assert that enabling an optional module produces a new migration and leaves InitialCreate unchanged. This boundary means a single InitialCreate is not a complete schema guarantee for projects that opt into generated modules; those require their own verified migrations.
